## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_1976
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Co"]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["tornado"]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["WELD", "KIOWA"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[202231800000000, 202234800000000]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["123", "61"]
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0", "0.5"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["33", "150"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["9986350", "9986355"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[0.0]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[202231800000000, 202234800000000]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["8"]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph) | eg=["F0", "F1"]

SND & KENAI MTNS", "BIG AND LITTLE WOOD RIVER REGION"]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["BROADCAST MEDIA", "GOVT OFFICIAL"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0, 5000]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["125", "31"]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["AFC", "PIH"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1077991200000000, 1077986700000000]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point | eg=["1"]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_1966
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["10055623", "10059035"]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph) | eg=["F0"]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["0", "33"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["27", "29"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-109651320000000, -110091600000000]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["thunderstorm wind", "tornado"]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Mi", "Ne"]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[55.0, 50.0]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-109651320000000, -110091600000000]
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0"]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["VERNON", "ROOSEVELT"]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["0", "217"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]

The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph) | eg=["F1", "F2"]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["hail", "tornado"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[71174400000000, 78858000000000]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["0", "50"]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["9983345", "10064819"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0, 250]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[1.75, 0.75]
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0", "0.1"]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["139", "79"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Ar", "Ne"]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["5", "31"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[71174400000000, 78858000000000]

state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["31", "19"]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["121", "1"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-298952340000000, -301203000000000]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_1998
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["avalanche"]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles)
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file | eg=["2072239", "2072547"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[885133800000000, 883843200000000]
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["MST", "PST"]

magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point | eg=["1"]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]

state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Mo", "Id"]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["5628077", "5627479"]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state. deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[1, 2]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[885133800000000, 883839600000000]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["TFX", "BOI"]

time: TEXT | Hour in UTC | eg=["2004", "2058"]
f_scale: TEXT | The F-Scale classifies F0-F5 damage as calibrated by engineers and meteorologists across 28 different types of damage indicators (mainly various kinds of buildings, but also a few other structures as well as trees). | eg=["UNK"]
latitude: FLOAT | Latitude to two decimal places | eg=[32.49, 32.85]
county: TEXT | NULL | eg=["AUTAUGA", "COOSA"]
comments: TEXT | NULL | eg=["*** 2 INJ *** CORRECTS PREVIOUS TORNADO REPORT TO INCLUDE 2 INJURIES. A NWS STORM SURVEY TEAM CONFIRMED A TORNADO OF EF-1 INTENSITY... WHICH OCCURRED JUST NORTHWEST OF (BMX)", "A NWS STORM SURVEY TEAM CONFIRMED A TORNADO OF EF-1 INTENSITY... AFFECTING AREAS JUST SOUTHEAST OF ROCKFORD IN COOSA COUNTY ALONG COUNTY ROAD 101. (BMX)"]

cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["COASTAL DUVAL", "COASTAL CUMBERLAND"]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state. State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["12", "23"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1600697700000000, 1600791120000000]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["921718", "922414"]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file | eg=["153056", "153192"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0, 5000]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["NWS Storm Survey", "Emergency Manager"]
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["astronomical low tide"]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["125", "24"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles)
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["EST-5", "CST-6"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1600695000000000, 1600785900000000]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Fl", "Ma"]
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["Z"]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["JAX", "GYX"]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood | eg=["nan"]

reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Co"]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["WASHINGTON", "DENVER"]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["T", "RY"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[302990400000000, 303679800000000]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[302990400000000, 303679800000000]

tor_f_scale: TEXT | "Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)" | eg=["F2"]

event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["hail", "thunderstorm wind"]
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]

The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph) | eg=["F3"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Al"]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["MONTGOMERY", "COLBERT"]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-42719580000000, -42627600000000]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["thunderstorm wind", "tornado"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["1"]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0", "12.2"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-42719580000000, -42627600000000]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0, 14]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0, 250000]
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.

State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["53", "8"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[1, 0]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0, 25000]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_1986
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[527458800000000, 530295720000000]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[527458800000000, 530295720000000]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["0"]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["WASHINGTON", "ARAPAHOE"]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["DEN", "STL"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["8", "29"]
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["hail", "thunderstorm wind"]
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["9986986", "9986987"]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[1.75, 0.75]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0"]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["121", "5"]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Co", "Mi"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]

magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[1.0, 1.75]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["101", "37"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0"]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-23011200000000, -16133100000000]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["NEWTON", "CASS"]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST"]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["9984356", "10057764"]

tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)

wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["SEW", "REV"]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["avalanche", "blizzard"]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.HAIL_REPORTS
time: TEXT | Hour in UTC | eg=["0820", "0840"]
location: TEXT | Location of report | eg=["1 SSW ORANGE BEACH", "4 SW LILLIAN"]
county: TEXT | NULL | eg=["BALDWIN", "BLOUNT"]
report_point: BINARY | NULL | eg=["bytearray(b'\\x01\\x01\\x00\\x00\\x00\\xf6(\\\\\\x8f\\xc2\\xe5U\\xc0\\x85\\xebQ\\xb8\\x1eE>@')", "bytearray(b'\\x01\\x01\\x00\\x00\\x00\\x1f\\x85\\xebQ\\xb8\\xdeU\\xc0\\xe1z\\x14\\xaeGa>@')"]
size: NUMBER | Size in 1/100 of an Inch (175 = 1.75in) | eg=[400, 200]
latitude: FLOAT | Latitude to two decimal places | eg=[30.27, 30.38]
comments: TEXT | NULL | eg=["TWO SEPARATE PHOTOS SHOW HAIL NEAR SOFTBALL SIZE IN ORANGE BEACH. SIGNIFICANT HAIL DAMAGE REPORTED TO NUMEROUS VEHICLES AND METALS ROOFS IN THE AREA. (MOB)", "REPORT OF 2 INCH SIZE HAIL FURTHER INLAND (SOUTHWEST OF LILLIAN). (MOB)"]
state: TEXT | NULL | eg=["AL"]
timestamp: NUMBER | Timestamp in UTC | eg=[1618042800000000, 1618044000000000]
longitude: FLOAT | Longitude to two decimal places | eg=[-87.59, -87.48]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_2017
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["PHI", "LCH"]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["astronomical low tide"]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["De", "Lo"]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state. State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["10", "22"]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["KENT", "EAST CAMERON"]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file | eg=["111462", "112915"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["2", "74"]
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1483650000000000, 1485126000000000]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood | eg=["nan"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1483682400000000, 1485169200000000]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles)
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["Trained Spotter", "C-MAN Station"]
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["Z"]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["665402", "675154"]
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["EST-5", "CST-6"]

event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["LCH"]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["astronomical low tide"]
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Te", "Lo"]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood | eg=["nan"]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["48", "22"]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["C-MAN Station"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["215", "54"]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined

event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["10070243", "10056221"]

wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["ATA", "LCO"]

flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood

damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]

tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)

event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[267903900000000, 276878100000000]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.

State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["22", "48"]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_2011
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["astronomical low tide"]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["EST-5"]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["273508", "273509"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1297749600000000, 1297753200000000]
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file | eg=["46994", "46995"]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["18", "19"]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["PHI"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1297738800000000, 1297742400000000]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["CAMDEN", "NORTHWESTERN BURLINGTON"]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles)
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state. State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["34", "10"]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood | eg=["nan"]
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Ne", "De"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["River/Stream Gage", "C-MAN Station"]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["Z"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)

state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["26", "10"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.

event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude

reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
