magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[772669200000000, 769009620000000]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[772669200000000, 769009620000000]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["49", "123"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["10314420", "10314036"]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["DEKALB", "TALLAPOOSA"]
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Al"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[50.0, 1.75]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["0"]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[500000, 0]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0"]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["thunderstorm wind", "hail"]

location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST", "EST"]
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[1.0, 0.0]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0, 3]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph) | eg=["F1", "F2"]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["27", "34"]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Mi", "Ne"]
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["POPE", "ETOWAH"]
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["0", "33"]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["hail", "thunderstorm wind"]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["10054407", "10079241"]

tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles) | eg=["0.0", "7.1"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0, 25000]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["121", "0"]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail

tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet) | eg=["0"]
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["MOBILE", "LIMESTONE"]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["1"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[-148385400000000, -150525900000000]
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured.    - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size.   Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches | eg=[2.0, 1.0]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["hail"]
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["97", "83"]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.

magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)

tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.

damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.WIND_REPORTS
comments: TEXT | NULL | eg=["DAMAGE TO A MOBILE HOME REPORTED BY EMA. PROBABLE TORNADO. (BMX)", "CORRECTS PREVIOUS TSTM WND DMG REPORT FROM PRATTVILLE. 5 TREES WERE SNAPPED OFF IN THE CAMELLIA ESTATES NEIGHBORHOOD IN PRATTVILLE. AT LEAST ONE RESIDENCE SUSTAINED ROO (BMX)"]
timestamp: NUMBER | Timestamp in UTC | eg=[1547929020000000, 1549989360000000]
report_point: BINARY | NULL | eg=["bytearray(b'\\x01\\x01\\x00\\x00\\x00fffff\\xa6U\\xc0\\x85\\xebQ\\xb8\\x1eE@@')", "bytearray(b'\\x01\\x01\\x00\\x00\\x00\\xcd\\xcc\\xcc\\xcc\\xcc\\x9cU\\xc0{\\x14\\xaeG\\xe1:@@')"]
location: TEXT | Location of report | eg=["3 NNW BOOTH", "PRATTVILLE"]
state: TEXT | NULL | eg=["AL"]
longitude: FLOAT | Longitude to two decimal places | eg=[-86.6, -86.45]
speed: NUMBER | Speed in Miles per hour | eg=[NaN, 61.0]
county: TEXT | NULL | eg=["AUTAUGA", "BALDWIN"]
latitude: FLOAT | Latitude to two decimal places | eg=[32.54, 32.46]
time: TEXT | Hour in UTC | eg=["2017", "1636"]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_2015
tor_length: TEXT | Length of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (minimal of tenths of miles)
event_type: TEXT | The only events permitted in Storm Data are listed in Table 1 of Section 2.1.1 of NWS Directive 10-1605 at http://www.nws.noaa.gov/directives/sym/pd01016005curr.pdf. The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["astronomical low tide"]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["LCH", "PHI"]
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to note a single, small part that goes into a specific storm episode; links the storm episode between the three files downloaded from SPC’s website | eg=["605426", "605427"]
state_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each state. State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.states_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_states | eg=["22", "10"]
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1445842800000000, 1451297700000000]
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["C-MAN Station", "WLON"]
magnitude: FLOAT | Measured extent of the magnitude type. This is only used for wind speeds and hail size. Wind speeds are in MPH; Hail sizes are in inches
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
cz_fips_code: TEXT | Unique FIPS code identifier assigned to each county.   State names and their corresponding FIPS codes are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.census_fips_codes.counties_2016  The geographic polygons that define the perimeter of each state are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.geo_us_boundaries.us_counties | eg=["73", "74"]
event_end_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event ended. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1445843700000000, 1451303100000000]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)
event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["CST-6", "EST-5"]
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["Z"]
episode_id: TEXT | ID assigned by NWS to denote the storm episode; links the event details file with the information within location file | eg=["101093", "102260"]
cz_name: TEXT | (County/Parish, Zone or Marine Name assigned to the county FIPS number or NWS Forecast Zone  NWS Forecast Zones are available as a BigQuery Public Dataset: bigquery-public-data.noaa_historic_severe_storms.nws_forecast_zones | eg=["WEST CAMERON", "EAST CAMERON"]
damage_property: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to property incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the event. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
flood_cause: TEXT | Reported or estimated cause of the flood | eg=["nan"]
magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Lo", "De"]
tor_other_wfo: TEXT | Indicates the continuation of a tornado segment as it crossed from one National Weather Service Forecast Office to another. The subsequent WFO identifier is provided within this field.
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.TORNADO_PATHS
magnitude: TEXT | EF-Scale rating for the tornado. Possible values are 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 , 5 and unknown | eg=["0", "1"]
storm_date: DATE | Date of the tornado | eg=["2017-03-06", "2008-04-10"]
time_zone_offset: TEXT | Timezone offset of where the the tornado occurred. Possible values are -06:00 (CST), -00:00 (GMT/UTC), and unknown | eg=["-06:00"]
end_lat: FLOAT | Tornado's end latitude in decimal degrees | eg=[41.4976, 40.7]
injured_count: NUMBER | Number of people injured as a result of the storm | eg=[0]
yearly_tornado_count: TEXT | Count of tornadoes that year after the given tornado is reported to the NWS database. This does not necessarily indicate sequential order because of reporting potential delays. | eg=["614739", "434"]
end_long: FLOAT | Tornado's end longitude in decimal degrees | eg=[-92.9041, -93.07]
storm_time: TIME | Time of the tornado | eg=["19:47:00", "15:57:00"]
length: FLOAT | Length of the tornado, in miles | eg=[5.45, 2.01]
crop_loss: TEXT | Estimated crop value loss in millions of USD. Note that zero does not indicate $0. This data was is available starting in 2007 | eg=["0", "5000"]
width: FLOAT | Width of the tornado in feet | eg=[195.0, 90.0]
start_lon: FLOAT | Tornado's starting longitude in decimal degrees | eg=[-92.9974, -93.08]
tornado_path_geom: BINARY | Geographic representation of the storm's path | eg=["bytearray(b'\\x01\\x02\\x00\\x00\\x00\\x02\\x00\\x00\\x00\\xf1A\\xcff\\xd5?W\\xc0\\xc2\\x17&S\\x05\\xbbD@\\x12\\x14?\\xc6\\xdc9W\\xc0\\xaa>W[\\xb1\\xbfD@')", "bytearray(b'\\x01\\x02\\x00\\x00\\x00\\x02\\x00\\x00\\x00\\x84\\xebQ\\xb8\\x1eEW\\xc0\\xf7(\\\\\\x8f\\xc2UD@\\x14\\xaeG\\xe1zDW\\xc0\\x9b\\x99\\x99\\x99\\x99YD@')"]
state_fips_code: TEXT | 2-digit state FIPS code. This maps to all state_fips_code columns in the BigQuery Public Datasets Program | eg=["19"]
state_abbreviation: TEXT | 2 letter postal abbreviation of the state's name | eg=["IA"]
fatality_count: NUMBER | Number of fatalities that resulted from the storm | eg=[0]
state_name: TEXT | Full text name of the state in which the tornado occurred | eg=["Iowa"]
start_lat: FLOAT | Tornado's starting latitude in decimal degrees | eg=[41.4611, 40.67]
property_loss: TEXT | Estimated property loss in USD. Note that zero does not indicate $0. Possible values are: 0 - unknown or not provided; 1 - <50; 2 - 50 - <500; 3 - 500 - <5,000; 4 - 5,000 - <50,000; 5 - 50,000 - <500,000; 6 - 500,000 - <5,000,000; 7 - 5,000,000 - <50,000,000; 8 - 50,000,000 - <500,000,000; 9 - 500,000,000 or greater. | eg=["25000", "0.075"]

The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["astronomical low tide"]
event_begin_time: NUMBER | The date and time that the event began. Note that episodes and events may have different start and end times if multiple events occured in the same episode | eg=[1611100800000000, 1612175400000000]
reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_point: BINARY | Geographic representation of the event_longitude and latitude
tor_width: TEXT | Width of the tornado or tornado segment while on the ground (in feet)
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories | eg=["CoCoRaHS", "C-MAN Station"]
injuries_indirect: NUMBER | The number of injuries indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
injuries_direct: NUMBER | The number of injuries directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]
tor_f_scale: TEXT | Enhanced Fujita Scale describes the strength of the tornado based on the amount and type of damage caused by the tornado. The F-scale of damage will vary in the destruction area; therefore, the highest value of the F-scale is recorded for each event.    - EF0 – Light Damage (40 – 72 mph)   - EF1 – Moderate Damage (73 – 112 mph)   - EF2 – Significant damage (113 – 157 mph)   - EF3 – Severe Damage (158 – 206 mph)   - EF4 – Devastating Damage (207 – 260 mph)   - EF5 – Incredible Damage (261 – 318 mph)
damage_crops: NUMBER | The estimated amount of damage to crops incurred by the weather event, in USD at the time of the storm. Values are not adjusted for inflation  Note: Values listed as 0 do not necessarily mean that no property damage occurred as a result of the event | eg=[0]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["PBZ", "LCH"]

## NOAA_HISTORIC_SEVERE_STORMS.STORMS_1992
cz_type: TEXT | Indicates whether the event happened in   - C: County/Parish  - Z: NWS zone  - M: Marine | eg=["C"]

reference_location: TEXT | Reference location of the center from which the range is calculated and the azimuth is determined
event_range: FLOAT | A hydro-meteorological event will be referenced, minimally, to the nearest tenth of a mile, to the geographical center (not from the village/city boundaries or limits) of a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database.
state: TEXT | The full text state name where the event occurred | eg=["Co"]

event_timezone: TEXT | The time zone in which the event_begin_time and the event_end_time is recorded. | eg=["PST", "MST"]

location_index: TEXT | Number assigned by NWS to specific locations within the same Storm event. Each event’s sequentially increasing location index number will have a corresponding lat/lon point
source: TEXT | Source reporting the weather event  Note: This can be any entry. Values are not restricted to specific categories
deaths_indirect: NUMBER | The number of deathes indirectly related to the weather event | eg=[0]

The chosen event type is the one that most accurately describes the meteorological event leading to fatalities, injuries, damage, etc. However, significant events, such as tornadoes, having no impact or causing no damage, are also included in Storm Data. | eg=["thunderstorm wind", "tornado"]
wfo: TEXT | National Weather Service Forecast Office’s area of responsibility (County Warning Area) in which the event occurred | eg=["DEN"]

magnitude_type: TEXT | Differentiates between the type of mangitude measured. - EG = Wind Estimated Gust  - ES = Estimated Sustained Wind  - MS = Measured Sustained Wind  - MG = Measured Wind Gust  No magnitude type is included for hail
deaths_direct: NUMBER | The number of deathes directly related to the weather event | eg=[0]

event_azimuth: TEXT | 16-point compass direction from a particular village/city, airport, or inland lake, providing that the reference point is documented in the Storm Data software location database of > 130,000 locations.
event_longitude: FLOAT | The longitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s W of the Prime Meridian)
event_latitude: FLOAT | The latitude where the event occurred (rounded to the hundredths in decimal degrees; includes an ‘-‘ if it’s S of the Equator)

## NOAA_ICOADS.ICOADS_CORE_2016
wave_height: FLOAT | Height of wind waves, in metres.

wave_direction: NUMBER | Starting in 1968, WD was no longer reported in the SHIP code. Codes 00 to 36 (note: leading zero is omitted in IMMA) show the direction (if any) from which (wind) waves come, in tens of degrees (following WMO Code 0877; ref. Code and Range columns in Table D2). Codes 37 and 38 show: 37 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (WH ≤ 4.75 m) 38 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (WH > 4.75 m; or irrespective of wave height, corresponding to 99 in WMO Code 0877)

ship_speed: NUMBER | Ship’s average speed made good during the three hours preceding the time of observation (beginning 1 January 1968): 0 – 0 knots  1 – 1-5 knots 2 – 6-10 knots 3 – 11-15 knots 4 – 16-20 knots  5 – 21-25 knots 6 – 26-30 knots 7 – 31-35 knots 8 – 36-40 knots 9 – over 40 knots. This also includes new data sources received, but awaiting blending into an official ICOADS Release) 2 – Full (A record included in an official ICOADS Release) | eg=[0]

dup_check: NUMBER | The presence of a duplicate match between a Global Telecommunication System (GTS) and logbook (or other delayed-mode) report may provide some location verification, with greater credibility if SLP and SST match under “allowances.” DUPC indicates whether such matches were detected during duplicate elimination processing (either the GTS or delayed-mode report is retained in the output data mixture), in case users might wish to make use of this information for independent quality control purposes: 0 – GTS and logbook match with SLP and SST match 1 – GTS and logbook match without SLP and SST match 2 – no GTS and logbook match was encountered | eg=[NaN, 2.0]

adaptive_qc_flags: TEXT | SQZ indicates the relationship of SST to “adaptive” QC limits in 0.5σ (standard deviation) increments, and SQZ provides a measure of the reliability of the QC | eg=[""]
second_country_code: NUMBER | The country of immediate receipt (C2), which may differ from the recruiting country (C1) and may also differ from the ship’s registry.
landlocked_flag: NUMBER | The single extant value of the landlocked flag (LZ) is defined as 1 = report over land If LZ is missing, this indicates that the report falls over an ocean or coastal region as defined by a “landlocked” file at 2°×2° resolution

dup_check: NUMBER | The presence of a duplicate match between a Global Telecommunication System (GTS) and logbook (or other delayed-mode) report may provide some location verification, with greater credibility if SLP and SST match under “allowances.” DUPC indicates whether such matches were detected during duplicate elimination processing (either the GTS or delayed-mode report is retained in the output data mixture), in case users might wish to make use of this information for independent quality control purposes: 0 – GTS and logbook match with SLP and SST match 1 – GTS and logbook match without SLP and SST match 2 – no GTS and logbook match was encountered | eg=[2]
swell_height: FLOAT | Height of wind waves, in metres.
release_status_indicator: NUMBER | An indicator that specifies whether the record is: 0 – Preliminary (Not yet included in an official ICOADS Release) 1 – Auxiliary (Records provided in separate data files in addition to ICOADS official Releases and Preliminary data. This also includes new data sources received, but awaiting blending into an official ICOADS Release) 2 – Full (A record included in an official ICOADS Release) | eg=[0]

For the letters, lower-case “x” indicates a flag value not utilized, whereas upper-case flag values were utilized, in preparing enhanced or standard trimmed data | eg=["1AAAA1AAA1AAA1"]

visibility: NUMBER | Visibility (horizontal visibility at the surface in kilometers) according to WMO Code 4377 from which, in reporting visibility at sea, WMO (2009a; Reg. 12.2.1.3.2) states that the decile 90-99 shall be used (moreover Reg. 12.2.1.3.1: when the horizontal visibility is not the same in different directions, the shortest distance shall be given for VV): 90 – less than 0.05 kilometer 91 – 0.05 92 – 0.2 93 – 0.5 94 – 1 95 – 2 96 – 4 97 – 10 98 – 20 99 – 50 or more
wave_direction: NUMBER | Starting in 1968, WD was no longer reported in the SHIP code. Codes 00 to 36 (note: leading zero is omitted in IMMA) show the direction (if any) from which (wind) waves come, in tens of degrees (following WMO Code 0877; ref. Code and Range columns in Table D2). Codes 37 and 38 show: 37 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (WH ≤ 4.75 m) 38 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (WH > 4.75 m; or irrespective of wave height, corresponding to 99 in WMO Code 0877)
wave_height: FLOAT | Height of wind waves, in metres.
wind_speed_indicator: NUMBER | Wind speed is stored in tenths of a meter per second (to retain adequate precision for winds converted from knots, or high-resolution data). WI shows the units in which and/or the method by which W was originally recorded (0, 1, 3, 4 follow WMO Code 1855): 0 – meter per second, estimated 53 1 – meter per second, obtained from anemometer (measured) 2 – estimated (original units unknown) 3 – knot, estimated 4 – knot, obtained from anemometer (measured) 5 – Beaufort force (based on documentation) 6 – estimated (original units unknown)/unknown method 7 – measured (original units unknown) 8 – high-resolution measurement (e.g., hundredths of a meter per second)
release_no_secondary: NUMBER | Second of the three elements that make up the full release number associated with the record. For example, Release 3.0.1 is represented with RN1=3, RN2=0, and RN3=1 | eg=[0]

platform_type: NUMBER | The type of observing platform: 0 – US Navy or “deck” log, or unknown 1 – merchant ship or foreign military 2 – ocean station vessel—off station or station proximity unknown 3 – ocean station vessel—on station 4 – lightship 5 – ship 6 – moored buoy 7 – drifting buoy 8 – ice buoy [note: currently unused] 9 – ice station (manned, including ships overwintering in ice) 10 – oceanographic station data (bottle and low-resolution CTD/XCTD data) 11 – mechanical/digital/micro bathythermograph (MBT) 12 – expendable bathythermograph (XBT) 13 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) (NDBC operated) 14 – other coastal/island station 15 – fixed (or mobile) ocean platform (plat, rig) 16 – tide gauge 17 – high-resolution Conductivity-Temp.-Depth (CTD)/Expendable CTD (XCTD) 74 18 – profiling float 19 – undulating oceanographic recorder 20 – autonomous pinneped bathythermograph 21 – glider | eg=[7]
id_indicator: NUMBER | ID Indicator indicates whether a callsign or some other sort of identification is contained in the ID field (and in R3.0 data, II should always be extant when ID information exists; whereas II should always be missing if ID is missing): 0 – ID present, but unknown type 1 – ship, Ocean Station Vessel (OSV), or ice station callsign 2 – generic ID (e.g., SHIP, BUOY, RIGG, PLAT) 3 – WMO 5-digit buoy number 4 – other buoy number (e.g., Argos or national buoy number) 5 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) ID (assigned by US NDBC or other organizations) 6 – station name or number 7 – oceanographic platform/cruise number 8 – fishing vessel psuedo-ID 9 – national ship number 10 – composite information from early ship data 11 – 7-digit buoy ID (proposed) | eg=[3]

WI shows the units in which and/or the method by which W was originally recorded (0, 1, 3, 4 follow WMO Code 1855): 0 – meter per second, estimated 53 1 – meter per second, obtained from anemometer (measured) 2 – estimated (original units unknown) 3 – knot, estimated 4 – knot, obtained from anemometer (measured) 5 – Beaufort force (based on documentation) 6 – estimated (original units unknown)/unknown method 7 – measured (original units unknown) 8 – high-resolution measurement (e.g., hundredths of a meter per second) | eg=[4]
second_country_code: NUMBER | The country of immediate receipt (C2), which may differ from the recruiting country (C1) and may also differ from the ship’s registry.
cloud_height: TEXT | Codes 0 to 9 and “A” (following WMO Code 1600) show the height above surface of the base of the lowest cloud seen (such that a height exactly equal to one of the values at  60 the ends of the ranges shall be coded in the higher range, e.g., a height of 600 m shall be reported by code 5): 0 – 0 to 50 m 1 – 50 to 100 m 2 – 100 to 200 m 3 – 200 to 300 m 4 – 300 to 600 m 5 – 600 to 1000 m 6 – 1000 to 1500 m 7 – 1500 to 2000 m 8 – 2000 to 2500 m 9 – 2500 m or more, or no clouds 10 [A in base36 encoding] – height of base of cloud not known or base of clouds at a level lower and tops at a level higher than that of the station | eg=["4", "5"]

indicator_for_temp: NUMBER | Indicator provides information about the precision and/or units that the Core temperature elements were translated from: 0 – tenths °C 1 – half °C 2 – whole °C 3 – whole or tenths °C (mixed precision among temperature fields) 4 – tenths °F 5 – half °F 6 – whole °F 7 – whole or tenths °F (mixed precision among temperature fields) 8 – high resolution data (e.g., hundredths °C) 9 – other | eg=[3]
low_cloud_type: TEXT | Codes 0 to 10 [A in base36 encoding] show characteristics observed of clouds of the genera Stratocumulus, Stratus, Cumulus, and Cumulonimbus (WMO Code 0513 | eg=[""]
swell_direction: NUMBER | Starting in 1968, SD was no longer reported in the SHIP code. Codes 00 to 36 (note: leading zero is omitted in IMMA) show the direction (if any) from which (wind) waves come, in tens of degrees (following WMO Code 0877; ref. Code and Range columns in Table D2). Codes 37 and 38 show: 37 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (SH ≤ 4.75 m) 38 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (SH > 4.75 m; or irrespective of wave height, corresponding to 99 in WMO Code 0877)
ship_speed: NUMBER | Ship’s average speed made good during the three hours preceding the time of observation (beginning 1 January 1968): 0 – 0 knots  1 – 1-5 knots 2 – 6-10 knots 3 – 11-15 knots 4 – 16-20 knots  5 – 21-25 knots 6 – 26-30 knots 7 – 31-35 knots 8 – 36-40 knots 9 – over 40 knots.    Prior to 1 January 1968 a different code for VS, also with range 0-9, applied (Met Office 1948): 0 – 0 knots 1 – 1-3 knots 2 – 4-6 knots 3 – 7-9 knots 4 – 10-12 knots 5 – 13-15 knots  6 – 16-18 knots 7 – 19-21 knots 8 – 22-24 knots 9 – over 24 knots
attm_count: NUMBER | The attm count: 0 – abbreviated record (no attm) 1 – one attm 2 – two attms etc. | eg=[3]
deck: NUMBER | Number of the deck from which the report came (Table D6a), with Tables D6b and D6c providing additional information about selected DCK ranges. “Deck” originally referred to a punched card deck, but is now used as the primary field to track ICOADS data collections. | eg=[714]
intermediate_reject_flag: NUMBER | 0=Retain in Intermediate, Reject from Final dataset;  1=Retain in both Intermediate and Final datasets;  2=Reject from both Intermediate and Final datasets | eg=[1]
dpt_indicator: NUMBER | WBTI and DPTI indicate which of WBT or DPT was measured or computed, and ice bulb conditions: 0 – measured 1 – computed 2 – iced measured 3 – iced computed
release_no_secondary: NUMBER | Second of the three elements that make up the full release number associated with the record. For example, Release 3.0.1 is represented with RN1=3, RN2=0, and RN3=1 | eg=[0]
day: NUMBER | Day of the actual time of observation at which the barometer is read. Days 1-31 | eg=[18, 15]
wave_period: NUMBER | Period of wind waves, in seconds. Starting in 1968, WP was reported in seconds; prior to 1968 the period was reported as a code, which was converted into whole seconds following Table D5a, with WX (C1, field 13) set accordingly.
middle_cloud_type: TEXT | Codes 0 to 10 [A in base36 encoding] show characteristics observed of clouds of the genera Altocumulus, Altostratus, and Nimbostratus (WMO Code 0515). | eg=[""]
wind_direction_true: NUMBER | The direction (true) from which wind is blowing (or will blow), stored in whole degrees (i.e., 360-point compass; range: 1-360°), or special codes: 361 – calm 362 – variable, or all directions
sea_surface_temp: FLOAT | Temperatures are stored in tenths of a degree Celsius. | eg=[18.8, 22.8]
cloud_height_indicator: NUMBER | Shows if cloud height H was: 0 – estimated 1 – measured
wbt_indicator: NUMBER | WBTI and DPTI indicate which of WBT or DPT was measured or computed, and ice bulb conditions: 0 – measured 1 – computed 2 – iced measured 3 – iced computed
characteristic_of_ppp: NUMBER | WMO Code 0200 for characteristic of pressure tendency during the three hours preceding the time of observation (A) (after WMO 2015).
sst_measurement_method: NUMBER | Shows the method by which SST was taken: 0 – bucket 1 – condenser inlet (intake) 2 – trailing thermistor 3 – hull contact sensor 4 – through hull sensor 5 – radiation thermometer 6 – bait tanks thermometer 7 – others 9 – unknown or non-bucket 10 – “implied” bucket [note: applicable to early ICOADS data] 11 – reversing thermometer or mechanical sensor 12 – electronic sensor
swell_period: NUMBER | Period of wind waves, in seconds. Starting in 1968, SP was reported in seconds; prior to 1968 the period was reported as a code, which was converted into whole seconds following Table D5a, with WX (C1, field 13) set accordingly.

timestamp: NUMBER | Converted UTC timestamp for the actual time of observation at which the barometer is read | eg=[1174183800000000, 1176671400000000]
imma_version: NUMBER | IMMA Version: 0=2010, 1=2016 | eg=[1]
visibility: NUMBER | Visibility (horizontal visibility at the surface in kilometers) according to WMO Code 4377 from which, in reporting visibility at sea, WMO (2009a; Reg. 12.2.1.3.2) states that the decile 90-99 shall be used (moreover Reg. 12.2.1.3.1: when the horizontal visibility is not the same in different directions, the shortest distance shall be given for VV): 90 – less than 0.05 kilometer 91 – 0.05 92 – 0.2 93 – 0.5 94 – 1 95 – 2 96 – 4 97 – 10 98 – 20 99 – 50 or more
total_cloud_amount: NUMBER | Codes 0 to 9 (WMO Code 2700) show the total fraction of the celestial dome covered by clouds (irrespective of their genus). 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena

wind_speed: FLOAT | Wind speed which is stored in tenths of a meter per second (to retain adequate precision for winds converted from knots, or high-resolution data)
callsign: TEXT | Identification/Callsign | eg=["51666"]

dewpoint_temperature: FLOAT | Dew-point temperature, Celsius
national_source_indicator: NUMBER | A field available for national use in identifying data subsets.
ncdc_qc_flags: TEXT | Possible NCDC-QC flag values, where “–” indicates an undefined flag value, or letters included in the table indicate a defined flag value. For the letters, lower-case “x” indicates a flag value not utilized, whereas upper-case flag values were utilized, in preparing enhanced or standard trimmed data | eg=["1AAAA1AAA1AAAA"]
adaptive_qc_flags: TEXT | SQZ indicates the relationship of SST to “adaptive” QC limits in 0.5σ (standard deviation) increments, and SQZ provides a measure of the reliability of the QC | eg=[""]
unique_report_id: TEXT | A unique ID for each record in ICOADS represented as a base36 number of length 6. | eg=["4NOKC7", "4OGCMX"]
high_cloud_type: TEXT | Codes 0 to 10 [A in base36 encoding] show characteristics observed of clouds of the genera Cirrus, Cirrocumulus and Cirrostratus (WMO Code 0509). | eg=[""]
cloud_height: TEXT | Codes 0 to 9 and “A” (following WMO Code 1600) show the height above surface of the base of the lowest cloud seen (such that a height exactly equal to one of the values at  60 the ends of the ranges shall be coded in the higher range, e.g., a height of 600 m shall be reported by code 5): 0 – 0 to 50 m 1 – 50 to 100 m 2 – 100 to 200 m 3 – 200 to 300 m 4 – 300 to 600 m 5 – 600 to 1000 m 6 – 1000 to 1500 m 7 – 1500 to 2000 m 8 – 2000 to 2500 m 9 – 2500 m or more, or no clouds 10 [A in base36 encoding] – height of base of cloud not known or base of clouds at a level lower and tops at a level higher than that of the station | eg=[""]
dup_check: NUMBER | The presence of a duplicate match between a Global Telecommunication System (GTS) and logbook (or other delayed-mode) report may provide some location verification, with greater credibility if SLP and SST match under “allowances.” DUPC indicates whether such matches were detected during duplicate elimination processing (either the GTS or delayed-mode report is retained in the output data mixture), in case users might wish to make use of this information for independent quality control purposes: 0 – GTS and logbook match with SLP and SST match 1 – GTS and logbook match without SLP and SST match 2 – no GTS and logbook match was encountered
wave_period_indicator: NUMBER | Indicates that the wave and swell periods were converted from code into whole seconds: 1 – period converted from code into whole seconds
past_weather: NUMBER | WMO Codes 4677 (Table D3) for WW, and 4561 for W1: 0 – Cloud covering 1/2 or less of the sky throughout the appropriate period 1 – Cloud covering more than 1/2 of the sky during part of the appropriate period and covering 1/2 or less during part of the period 2 – Cloud covering more than 1/2 of the sky throughout the appropriate period 54 3 – Sandstorm, duststorm or blowing snow 4 – Fog or ice fog or thick haze 5 – Drizzle 6 – Rain 7 – Snow, or rain and snow mixed 8 – Shower(s) 9 – Thunderstorm(s) with or without precipitation

swell_period_indicator: NUMBER | Indicates that the wave and swell periods were converted from code into whole seconds: 1 – period converted from code into whole seconds

platform_type: NUMBER | The type of observing platform: 0 – US Navy or “deck” log, or unknown 1 – merchant ship or foreign military 2 – ocean station vessel—off station or station proximity unknown 3 – ocean station vessel—on station 4 – lightship 5 – ship 6 – moored buoy 7 – drifting buoy 8 – ice buoy [note: currently unused] 9 – ice station (manned, including ships overwintering in ice) 10 – oceanographic station data (bottle and low-resolution CTD/XCTD data) 11 – mechanical/digital/micro bathythermograph (MBT) 12 – expendable bathythermograph (XBT) 13 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) (NDBC operated) 14 – other coastal/island station 15 – fixed (or mobile) ocean platform (plat, rig) 16 – tide gauge 17 – high-resolution Conductivity-Temp.-Depth (CTD)/Expendable CTD (XCTD) 74 18 – profiling float 19 – undulating oceanographic recorder 20 – autonomous pinneped bathythermograph 21 – glider | eg=[7]

id_indicator: NUMBER | ID Indicator indicates whether a callsign or some other sort of identification is contained in the ID field (and in R3.0 data, II should always be extant when ID information exists; whereas II should always be missing if ID is missing): 0 – ID present, but unknown type 1 – ship, Ocean Station Vessel (OSV), or ice station callsign 2 – generic ID (e.g., SHIP, BUOY, RIGG, PLAT) 3 – WMO 5-digit buoy number 4 – other buoy number (e.g., Argos or national buoy number) 5 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) ID (assigned by US NDBC or other organizations) 6 – station name or number 7 – oceanographic platform/cruise number 8 – fishing vessel psuedo-ID 9 – national ship number 10 – composite information from early ship data 11 – 7-digit buoy ID (proposed) | eg=[3]
landlocked_flag: NUMBER | The single extant value of the landlocked flag (LZ) is defined as 1 = report over land If LZ is missing, this indicates that the report falls over an ocean or coastal region as defined by a “landlocked” file at 2°×2° resolution
platform_type: NUMBER | The type of observing platform: 0 – US Navy or “deck” log, or unknown 1 – merchant ship or foreign military 2 – ocean station vessel—off station or station proximity unknown 3 – ocean station vessel—on station 4 – lightship 5 – ship 6 – moored buoy 7 – drifting buoy 8 – ice buoy [note: currently unused] 9 – ice station (manned, including ships overwintering in ice) 10 – oceanographic station data (bottle and low-resolution CTD/XCTD data) 11 – mechanical/digital/micro bathythermograph (MBT) 12 – expendable bathythermograph (XBT) 13 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) (NDBC operated) 14 – other coastal/island station 15 – fixed (or mobile) ocean platform (plat, rig) 16 – tide gauge 17 – high-resolution Conductivity-Temp.-Depth (CTD)/Expendable CTD (XCTD) 74 18 – profiling float 19 – undulating oceanographic recorder 20 – autonomous pinneped bathythermograph 21 – glider | eg=[7]
adaptive_qc_flags: TEXT | SQZ indicates the relationship of SST to “adaptive” QC limits in 0.5σ (standard deviation) increments, and SQZ provides a measure of the reliability of the QC | eg=[""]
wave_height: FLOAT | Height of wind waves, in metres.
high_cloud_type: TEXT | Codes 0 to 10 [A in base36 encoding] show characteristics observed of clouds of the genera Cirrus, Cirrocumulus and Cirrostratus (WMO Code 0509). | eg=[""]
year: NUMBER | Year of the actual time of observation at which the barometer is read. It is 4-digit | eg=[2014]
low_cloud_type: TEXT | Codes 0 to 10 [A in base36 encoding] show characteristics observed of clouds of the genera Stratocumulus, Stratus, Cumulus, and Cumulonimbus (WMO Code 0513 | eg=[""]
dup_check: NUMBER | The presence of a duplicate match between a Global Telecommunication System (GTS) and logbook (or other delayed-mode) report may provide some location verification, with greater credibility if SLP and SST match under “allowances.” DUPC indicates whether such matches were detected during duplicate elimination processing (either the GTS or delayed-mode report is retained in the output data mixture), in case users might wish to make use of this information for independent quality control purposes: 0 – GTS and logbook match with SLP and SST match 1 – GTS and logbook match without SLP and SST match 2 – no GTS and logbook match was encountered
wetbulb_temperature: FLOAT | Wet-bulb temperature, Celsius
dewpoint_temperature: FLOAT | Dew-point temperature, Celsius
wave_period: NUMBER | Period of wind waves, in seconds. Starting in 1968, WP was reported in seconds; prior to 1968 the period was reported as a code, which was converted into whole seconds following Table D5a, with WX (C1, field 13) set accordingly.
lower_cloud_amount: NUMBER | 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena
release_status_indicator: NUMBER | An indicator that specifies whether the record is: 0 – Preliminary (Not yet included in an official ICOADS Release) 1 – Auxiliary (Records provided in separate data files in addition to ICOADS official Releases and Preliminary data. This also includes new data sources received, but awaiting blending into an official ICOADS Release) 2 – Full (A record included in an official ICOADS Release) | eg=[2]
air_temperature: FLOAT | Air temperature, Celsius
sea_surface_temp: FLOAT | Temperatures are stored in tenths of a degree Celsius. | eg=[23.3, 22.1]

dup_status: NUMBER | Indicates duplicate status. 0 unique 1 best duplicate 2 best duplicate with substitution | eg=[0]
callsign: TEXT | Identification/Callsign | eg=["44613"]
cloud_height: TEXT | Codes 0 to 9 and “A” (following WMO Code 1600) show the height above surface of the base of the lowest cloud seen (such that a height exactly equal to one of the values at  60 the ends of the ranges shall be coded in the higher range, e.g., a height of 600 m shall be reported by code 5): 0 – 0 to 50 m 1 – 50 to 100 m 2 – 100 to 200 m 3 – 200 to 300 m 4 – 300 to 600 m 5 – 600 to 1000 m 6 – 1000 to 1500 m 7 – 1500 to 2000 m 8 – 2000 to 2500 m 9 – 2500 m or more, or no clouds 10 [A in base36 encoding] – height of base of cloud not known or base of clouds at a level lower and tops at a level higher than that of the station | eg=[""]

sst_measurement_method: NUMBER | Shows the method by which SST was taken: 0 – bucket 1 – condenser inlet (intake) 2 – trailing thermistor 3 – hull contact sensor 4 – through hull sensor 5 – radiation thermometer 6 – bait tanks thermometer 7 – others 9 – unknown or non-bucket 10 – “implied” bucket [note: applicable to early ICOADS data] 11 – reversing thermometer or mechanical sensor 12 – electronic sensor

year: NUMBER | Year of the actual time of observation at which the barometer is read. It is 4-digit | eg=[2017]
cloud_height: TEXT | Codes 0 to 9 and “A” (following WMO Code 1600) show the height above surface of the base of the lowest cloud seen (such that a height exactly equal to one of the values at  60 the ends of the ranges shall be coded in the higher range, e.g., a height of 600 m shall be reported by code 5): 0 – 0 to 50 m 1 – 50 to 100 m 2 – 100 to 200 m 3 – 200 to 300 m 4 – 300 to 600 m 5 – 600 to 1000 m 6 – 1000 to 1500 m 7 – 1500 to 2000 m 8 – 2000 to 2500 m 9 – 2500 m or more, or no clouds 10 [A in base36 encoding] – height of base of cloud not known or base of clouds at a level lower and tops at a level higher than that of the station | eg=[""]
deck: NUMBER | Number of the deck from which the report came (Table D6a), with Tables D6b and D6c providing additional information about selected DCK ranges. “Deck” originally referred to a punched card deck, but is now used as the primary field to track ICOADS data collections. | eg=[794]
cloud_height_indicator: NUMBER | Shows if cloud height H was: 0 – estimated 1 – measured

longitude: FLOAT | Position to hundredths of a degree +N or –S (measured north or south of the equator) and +E or −W (measured east or west of the Greenwich Meridian). | eg=[102.2, 100.11]
dup_check: NUMBER | The presence of a duplicate match between a Global Telecommunication System (GTS) and logbook (or other delayed-mode) report may provide some location verification, with greater credibility if SLP and SST match under “allowances.” DUPC indicates whether such matches were detected during duplicate elimination processing (either the GTS or delayed-mode report is retained in the output data mixture), in case users might wish to make use of this information for independent quality control purposes: 0 – GTS and logbook match with SLP and SST match 1 – GTS and logbook match without SLP and SST match 2 – no GTS and logbook match was encountered | eg=[2]

track_check: NUMBER | Indicates if a report was: 0 – not track checked 1 – track checked

ship_course: NUMBER | True direction of resultant displacement of the ship during the three hours preceding the time of observation (i.e., ship’s course (true) made good): 0 – stationary (ship hove to) 5 – SW 1 – NE 6 – W 2 – E 7 – NW 3 – SE 8 – N 4 – S 9 – unknown
indicator_for_temp: NUMBER | Indicator provides information about the precision and/or units that the Core temperature elements were translated from: 0 – tenths °C 1 – half °C 2 – whole °C 3 – whole or tenths °C (mixed precision among temperature fields) 4 – tenths °F 5 – half °F 6 – whole °F 7 – whole or tenths °F (mixed precision among temperature fields) 8 – high resolution data (e.g., hundredths °C) 9 – other | eg=[0]

Codes 37 and 38 show: 37 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (WH ≤ 4.75 m) 38 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (WH > 4.75 m; or irrespective of wave height, corresponding to 99 in WMO Code 0877)

total_cloud_amount: NUMBER | Codes 0 to 9 (WMO Code 2700) show the total fraction of the celestial dome covered by clouds (irrespective of their genus). 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena
id_indicator: NUMBER | ID Indicator indicates whether a callsign or some other sort of identification is contained in the ID field (and in R3.0 data, II should always be extant when ID information exists; whereas II should always be missing if ID is missing): 0 – ID present, but unknown type 1 – ship, Ocean Station Vessel (OSV), or ice station callsign 2 – generic ID (e.g., SHIP, BUOY, RIGG, PLAT) 3 – WMO 5-digit buoy number 4 – other buoy number (e.g., Argos or national buoy number) 5 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) ID (assigned by US NDBC or other organizations) 6 – station name or number 7 – oceanographic platform/cruise number 8 – fishing vessel psuedo-ID 9 – national ship number 10 – composite information from early ship data 11 – 7-digit buoy ID (proposed) | eg=[3]

lower_cloud_amount: NUMBER | 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena
external: NUMBER | The single extant value of the external flags is defined as 1 = erroneous (based on external OSD buoy quality control) They range from 0-63

wind_speed_indicator: NUMBER | Wind speed is stored in tenths of a meter per second (to retain adequate precision for winds converted from knots, or high-resolution data). WI shows the units in which and/or the method by which W was originally recorded (0, 1, 3, 4 follow WMO Code 1855): 0 – meter per second, estimated 53 1 – meter per second, obtained from anemometer (measured) 2 – estimated (original units unknown) 3 – knot, estimated 4 – knot, obtained from anemometer (measured) 5 – Beaufort force (based on documentation) 6 – estimated (original units unknown)/unknown method 7 – measured (original units unknown) 8 – high-resolution measurement (e.g., hundredths of a meter per second)

For the letters, lower-case “x” indicates a flag value not utilized, whereas upper-case flag values were utilized, in preparing enhanced or standard trimmed data | eg=["1AAAA1AAA1AAAA"]
platform_type: NUMBER | The type of observing platform: 0 – US Navy or “deck” log, or unknown 1 – merchant ship or foreign military 2 – ocean station vessel—off station or station proximity unknown 3 – ocean station vessel—on station 4 – lightship 5 – ship 6 – moored buoy 7 – drifting buoy 8 – ice buoy [note: currently unused] 9 – ice station (manned, including ships overwintering in ice) 10 – oceanographic station data (bottle and low-resolution CTD/XCTD data) 11 – mechanical/digital/micro bathythermograph (MBT) 12 – expendable bathythermograph (XBT) 13 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) (NDBC operated) 14 – other coastal/island station 15 – fixed (or mobile) ocean platform (plat, rig) 16 – tide gauge 17 – high-resolution Conductivity-Temp.-Depth (CTD)/Expendable CTD (XCTD) 74 18 – profiling float 19 – undulating oceanographic recorder 20 – autonomous pinneped bathythermograph 21 – glider | eg=[7]

cloud_height: TEXT | Codes 0 to 9 and “A” (following WMO Code 1600) show the height above surface of the base of the lowest cloud seen (such that a height exactly equal to one of the values at  60 the ends of the ranges shall be coded in the higher range, e.g., a height of 600 m shall be reported by code 5): 0 – 0 to 50 m 1 – 50 to 100 m 2 – 100 to 200 m 3 – 200 to 300 m 4 – 300 to 600 m 5 – 600 to 1000 m 6 – 1000 to 1500 m 7 – 1500 to 2000 m 8 – 2000 to 2500 m 9 – 2500 m or more, or no clouds 10 [A in base36 encoding] – height of base of cloud not known or base of clouds at a level lower and tops at a level higher than that of the station | eg=[""]
adaptive_qc_flags: TEXT | SQZ indicates the relationship of SST to “adaptive” QC limits in 0.5σ (standard deviation) increments, and SQZ provides a measure of the reliability of the QC | eg=[""]
wave_period_indicator: NUMBER | Indicates that the wave and swell periods were converted from code into whole seconds: 1 – period converted from code into whole seconds
hour: FLOAT | Hour of the actual time of observation at which the barometer is read. Hours 0.00 to 23.99 | eg=[3.8, 3.17]
wind_speed_indicator: NUMBER | Wind speed is stored in tenths of a meter per second (to retain adequate precision for winds converted from knots, or high-resolution data). WI shows the units in which and/or the method by which W was originally recorded (0, 1, 3, 4 follow WMO Code 1855): 0 – meter per second, estimated 53 1 – meter per second, obtained from anemometer (measured) 2 – estimated (original units unknown) 3 – knot, estimated 4 – knot, obtained from anemometer (measured) 5 – Beaufort force (based on documentation) 6 – estimated (original units unknown)/unknown method 7 – measured (original units unknown) 8 – high-resolution measurement (e.g., hundredths of a meter per second)
lower_cloud_amount: NUMBER | 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena
unique_report_id: TEXT | A unique ID for each record in ICOADS represented as a base36 number of length 6. | eg=["I5YK9C", "I7LW0M"]
indicator_for_temp: NUMBER | Indicator provides information about the precision and/or units that the Core temperature elements were translated from: 0 – tenths °C 1 – half °C 2 – whole °C 3 – whole or tenths °C (mixed precision among temperature fields) 4 – tenths °F 5 – half °F 6 – whole °F 7 – whole or tenths °F (mixed precision among temperature fields) 8 – high resolution data (e.g., hundredths °C) 9 – other | eg=[3]
box_system_indicator: TEXT | The box system indicator is currently unused. | eg=[""]
release_status_indicator: NUMBER | An indicator that specifies whether the record is: 0 – Preliminary (Not yet included in an official ICOADS Release) 1 – Auxiliary (Records provided in separate data files in addition to ICOADS official Releases and Preliminary data. This also includes new data sources received, but awaiting blending into an official ICOADS Release) 2 – Full (A record included in an official ICOADS Release) | eg=[2]
pressure_bias: NUMBER | Indicates questionable sea level pressure data: 0 – questionable SLP: level 0: individual platform (unused) 1 – questionable SLP: level 1: deck 2 – questionable SLP: level 2: deck
latlong_indicator: NUMBER | Latlong indicator preserves the precision at which LAT and LON were recorded or translated from, or if they were derived later by interpolation between known positions: 0 – degrees and tenths 1 – whole degrees 2 – mixed precision 3 – interpolated 4 – degrees and minutes 5 – high resolution data (e.g., degrees to seconds) 6 – other | eg=[5]
trimming_flags: TEXT | These flags indicate the relationship of a given observational data value to the legacy trimming limits, or to indicate if those limits, which were calculated separately for three historical periods are unavailable, or other conditions. | eg=["1FFFFF"]

## NOAA_ICOADS.ICOADS_CORE_2006
id_indicator: NUMBER | ID Indicator indicates whether a callsign or some other sort of identification is contained in the ID field (and in R3.0 data, II should always be extant when ID information exists; whereas II should always be missing if ID is missing): 0 – ID present, but unknown type 1 – ship, Ocean Station Vessel (OSV), or ice station callsign 2 – generic ID (e.g., SHIP, BUOY, RIGG, PLAT) 3 – WMO 5-digit buoy number 4 – other buoy number (e.g., Argos or national buoy number) 5 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) ID (assigned by US NDBC or other organizations) 6 – station name or number 7 – oceanographic platform/cruise number 8 – fishing vessel psuedo-ID 9 – national ship number 10 – composite information from early ship data 11 – 7-digit buoy ID (proposed) | eg=[3]

past_weather: NUMBER | WMO Codes 4677 (Table D3) for WW, and 4561 for W1: 0 – Cloud covering 1/2 or less of the sky throughout the appropriate period 1 – Cloud covering more than 1/2 of the sky during part of the appropriate period and covering 1/2 or less during part of the period 2 – Cloud covering more than 1/2 of the sky throughout the appropriate period 54 3 – Sandstorm, duststorm or blowing snow 4 – Fog or ice fog or thick haze 5 – Drizzle 6 – Rain 7 – Snow, or rain and snow mixed 8 – Shower(s) 9 – Thunderstorm(s) with or without precipitation

wbt_indicator: NUMBER | WBTI and DPTI indicate which of WBT or DPT was measured or computed, and ice bulb conditions: 0 – measured 1 – computed 2 – iced measured 3 – iced computed
track_check: NUMBER | Indicates if a report was: 0 – not track checked 1 – track checked
swell_direction: NUMBER | Starting in 1968, SD was no longer reported in the SHIP code. Codes 00 to 36 (note: leading zero is omitted in IMMA) show the direction (if any) from which (wind) waves come, in tens of degrees (following WMO Code 0877; ref. Code and Range columns in Table D2). Codes 37 and 38 show: 37 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (SH ≤ 4.75 m) 38 – waves confused, direction indeterminate (SH > 4.75 m; or irrespective of wave height, corresponding to 99 in WMO Code 0877)
lower_cloud_amount: NUMBER | 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena
wind_speed: FLOAT | Wind speed which is stored in tenths of a meter per second (to retain adequate precision for winds converted from knots, or high-resolution data)
landlocked_flag: NUMBER | The single extant value of the landlocked flag (LZ) is defined as 1 = report over land If LZ is missing, this indicates that the report falls over an ocean or coastal region as defined by a “landlocked” file at 2°×2° resolution
attm_count: NUMBER | The attm count: 0 – abbreviated record (no attm) 1 – one attm 2 – two attms etc. | eg=[3]

wave_period: NUMBER | Period of wind waves, in seconds. Starting in 1968, WP was reported in seconds; prior to 1968 the period was reported as a code, which was converted into whole seconds following Table D5a, with WX (C1, field 13) set accordingly.

cloud_height: TEXT | Codes 0 to 9 and “A” (following WMO Code 1600) show the height above surface of the base of the lowest cloud seen (such that a height exactly equal to one of the values at  60 the ends of the ranges shall be coded in the higher range, e.g., a height of 600 m shall be reported by code 5): 0 – 0 to 50 m 1 – 50 to 100 m 2 – 100 to 200 m 3 – 200 to 300 m 4 – 300 to 600 m 5 – 600 to 1000 m 6 – 1000 to 1500 m 7 – 1500 to 2000 m 8 – 2000 to 2500 m 9 – 2500 m or more, or no clouds 10 [A in base36 encoding] – height of base of cloud not known or base of clouds at a level lower and tops at a level higher than that of the station | eg=["5", "4"]
ncdc_qc_flags: TEXT | Possible NCDC-QC flag values, where “–” indicates an undefined flag value, or letters included in the table indicate a defined flag value. For the letters, lower-case “x” indicates a flag value not utilized, whereas upper-case flag values were utilized, in preparing enhanced or standard trimmed data | eg=["11111111111111"]
wind_direction_true: NUMBER | The direction (true) from which wind is blowing (or will blow), stored in whole degrees (i.e., 360-point compass; range: 1-360°), or special codes: 361 – calm 362 – variable, or all directions | eg=[100, 130]
low_cloud_type: TEXT | Codes 0 to 10 [A in base36 encoding] show characteristics observed of clouds of the genera Stratocumulus, Stratus, Cumulus, and Cumulonimbus (WMO Code 0513 | eg=["2", "4"]
landlocked_flag: NUMBER | The single extant value of the landlocked flag (LZ) is defined as 1 = report over land If LZ is missing, this indicates that the report falls over an ocean or coastal region as defined by a “landlocked” file at 2°×2° resolution
lower_cloud_amount: NUMBER | 0 – clear 1 – 1 okta or less, but not zero 2-6 – 2-6 oktas 7 – 7 oktas or more, but not 8 oktas 8 – 8 oktas 9 – sky obscured by fog and/or other meteorological phenomena | eg=[4, 5]
swell_period: NUMBER | Period of wind waves, in seconds. Starting in 1968, SP was reported in seconds; prior to 1968 the period was reported as a code, which was converted into whole seconds following Table D5a, with WX (C1, field 13) set accordingly. | eg=[7, 8]
wbt_indicator: NUMBER | WBTI and DPTI indicate which of WBT or DPT was measured or computed, and ice bulb conditions: 0 – measured 1 – computed 2 – iced measured 3 – iced computed | eg=[0]
hour: FLOAT | Hour of the actual time of observation at which the barometer is read. Hours 0.00 to 23.99 | eg=[18.0, 21.0]
platform_type: NUMBER | The type of observing platform: 0 – US Navy or “deck” log, or unknown 1 – merchant ship or foreign military 2 – ocean station vessel—off station or station proximity unknown 3 – ocean station vessel—on station 4 – lightship 5 – ship 6 – moored buoy 7 – drifting buoy 8 – ice buoy [note: currently unused] 9 – ice station (manned, including ships overwintering in ice) 10 – oceanographic station data (bottle and low-resolution CTD/XCTD data) 11 – mechanical/digital/micro bathythermograph (MBT) 12 – expendable bathythermograph (XBT) 13 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) (NDBC operated) 14 – other coastal/island station 15 – fixed (or mobile) ocean platform (plat, rig) 16 – tide gauge 17 – high-resolution Conductivity-Temp.-Depth (CTD)/Expendable CTD (XCTD) 74 18 – profiling float 19 – undulating oceanographic recorder 20 – autonomous pinneped bathythermograph 21 – glider | eg=[5]

national_source_indicator: NUMBER | A field available for national use in identifying data subsets.

visibility: NUMBER | Visibility (horizontal visibility at the surface in kilometers) according to WMO Code 4377 from which, in reporting visibility at sea, WMO (2009a; Reg. 12.2.1.3.2) states that the decile 90-99 shall be used (moreover Reg. 12.2.1.3.1: when the horizontal visibility is not the same in different directions, the shortest distance shall be given for VV): 90 – less than 0.05 kilometer 91 – 0.05 92 – 0.2 93 – 0.5 94 – 1 95 – 2 96 – 4 97 – 10 98 – 20 99 – 50 or more | eg=[97]
latlong_indicator: NUMBER | Latlong indicator preserves the precision at which LAT and LON were recorded or translated from, or if they were derived later by interpolation between known positions: 0 – degrees and tenths 1 – whole degrees 2 – mixed precision 3 – interpolated 4 – degrees and minutes 5 – high resolution data (e.g., degrees to seconds) 6 – other | eg=[0]
past_weather: NUMBER | WMO Codes 4677 (Table D3) for WW, and 4561 for W1: 0 – Cloud covering 1/2 or less of the sky throughout the appropriate period 1 – Cloud covering more than 1/2 of the sky during part of the appropriate period and covering 1/2 or less during part of the period 2 – Cloud covering more than 1/2 of the sky throughout the appropriate period 54 3 – Sandstorm, duststorm or blowing snow 4 – Fog or ice fog or thick haze 5 – Drizzle 6 – Rain 7 – Snow, or rain and snow mixed 8 – Shower(s) 9 – Thunderstorm(s) with or without precipitation | eg=[1, 6]

id_indicator: NUMBER | ID Indicator indicates whether a callsign or some other sort of identification is contained in the ID field (and in R3.0 data, II should always be extant when ID information exists; whereas II should always be missing if ID is missing): 0 – ID present, but unknown type 1 – ship, Ocean Station Vessel (OSV), or ice station callsign 2 – generic ID (e.g., SHIP, BUOY, RIGG, PLAT) 3 – WMO 5-digit buoy number 4 – other buoy number (e.g., Argos or national buoy number) 5 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) ID (assigned by US NDBC or other organizations) 6 – station name or number 7 – oceanographic platform/cruise number 8 – fishing vessel psuedo-ID 9 – national ship number 10 – composite information from early ship data 11 – 7-digit buoy ID (proposed) | eg=[1]
release_status_indicator: NUMBER | An indicator that specifies whether the record is: 0 – Preliminary (Not yet included in an official ICOADS Release) 1 – Auxiliary (Records provided in separate data files in addition to ICOADS official Releases and Preliminary data. visibility: NUMBER | Visibility (horizontal visibility at the surface in kilometers) according to WMO Code 4377 from which, in reporting visibility at sea, WMO (2009a; Reg. 12.2.1.3.2) states that the decile 90-99 shall be used (moreover Reg. 12.2.1.3.1: when the horizontal visibility is not the same in different directions, the shortest distance shall be given for VV): 90 – less than 0.05 kilometer 91 – 0.05 92 – 0.2 93 – 0.5 94 – 1 95 – 2 96 – 4 97 – 10 98 – 20 99 – 50 or more
wave_period: NUMBER | Period of wind waves, in seconds. Starting in 1968, WP was reported in seconds; prior to 1968 the period was reported as a code, which was converted into whole seconds following Table D5a, with WX (C1, field 13) set accordingly.
release_status_indicator: NUMBER | An indicator that specifies whether the record is: 0 – Preliminary (Not yet included in an official ICOADS Release) 1 – Auxiliary (Records provided in separate data files in addition to ICOADS official Releases and Preliminary data. This also includes new data sources received, but awaiting blending into an official ICOADS Release) 2 – Full (A record included in an official ICOADS Release) | eg=[2]

platform_type: NUMBER | The type of observing platform: 0 – US Navy or “deck” log, or unknown 1 – merchant ship or foreign military 2 – ocean station vessel—off station or station proximity unknown 3 – ocean station vessel—on station 4 – lightship 5 – ship 6 – moored buoy 7 – drifting buoy 8 – ice buoy [note: currently unused] 9 – ice station (manned, including ships overwintering in ice) 10 – oceanographic station data (bottle and low-resolution CTD/XCTD data) 11 – mechanical/digital/micro bathythermograph (MBT) 12 – expendable bathythermograph (XBT) 13 – Coastal-Marine Automated Network (C-MAN) (NDBC operated) 14 – other coastal/island station 15 – fixed (or mobile) ocean platform (plat, rig) 16 – tide gauge 17 – high-resolution Conductivity-Temp.-Depth (CTD)/Expendable CTD (XCTD) 74 18 – profiling float 19 – undulating oceanographic recorder 20 – autonomous pinneped bathythermograph 21 – glider | eg=[7]
visibility: NUMBER | Visibility (horizontal visibility at the surface in kilometers) according to WMO Code 4377 from which, in reporting visibility at sea, WMO (2009a; Reg. 12.2.1.3.2) states that the decile 90-99 shall be used (moreover Reg. 12.2.1.3.1: when the horizontal visibility is not the same in different directions, the shortest distance shall be given for VV): 90 – less than 0.05 kilometer 91 – 0.05 92 – 0.2 93 – 0.5 94 – 1 95 – 2 96 – 4 97 – 10 98 – 20 99 – 50 or more
